data.title: Motorola Solutions: Paying Up For Quality As Silvus Unlocks Growth
data.description:
Preamble

In my most recent piece, I examined the 37 articles that I had published on Seeking Alpha in an attempt to learn from them. In the process, I was able to distill my investment philosophy into a formalized approach, one which I will employ in studying the following company, Motorola Solutions, Inc. (MSI).

Moving forward, this is how I intend to analyze companies, to reach a decision that fits my world view as an investor who values risk management before returns.

Let's start.

Level 1 Analysis

Business: What is MSI's value proposition?

According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

Motorola Solutions' business is safety and security. Every day we work to deliver on our commitment of helping to create safer communities, safer schools, safer hospitals and safer businesses...

We are building and connecting an ecosystem of safety and security technologies to help protect people, property and places, which includes Land Mobile Radio Communications ("LMR" or "LMR Communications"), Video Security and Access Control ("Video") and Command Center...

We support police, fire and other emergency responders to help protect communities, while our base of enterprise customers, including schools, hospitals, businesses and stadiums, continues to grow as the criticality of safety and security becomes increasingly important.

A check on MSI's website confirms this. MSI's products and services address mission-critical needs in numerous industries:

MSI sells communication devices that operate under extreme conditions.

MSI offers video solutions, be it fixed or mobile (body-worn cameras, in-car video systems) to allow its clients to "respond to events with speed and decisiveness to keep [their] people and property safe."

MSI does not just sell hardware. Instead, the different hardware is connected to its Command Centre that integrates audio, visual and data feeds and uses artificial intelligence to generate a fuller insight into a situation so security personnel/team could make informed decisions with greater clarity, certainty and speed.

What are MSI's revenue streams?

According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

We manage our business organizationally through two segments: “Products and Systems Integration” and “Software and Services.”

Within these segments, we have three principal product lines in which we report net sales:

•LMR Communications (MCN from Q2 2025 onwards): Infrastructure, devices (two-way radio and broadband, including both for public safety and professional and commercial radio ("PCR")) and software that enable communications, inclusive of installation and integration, backed by services, to assure availability, security and resiliency;

•Video: Cameras (fixed, body-worn, in-vehicle), access control, sensors, infrastructure, video management, software and artificial intelligence ("AI")-powered analytics that help enable visibility and bring attention to what’s important; and

•Command Center: Command center solutions and software applications that unify voice, video, data and analytics from public safety agencies, enterprises and the community to create a broad informational view to help simplify workflows and improve the accuracy and speed of decisions.

Which is the larger revenue segment?

The Products and Systems Integration segment is the larger of the two. In FY 2024, this segment was responsible for 64% of MSI's total sales, bringing in $6.9 billion.

The Software and Services segment delivered $3.9 billion, 36% of MSI total sales. There are three sub-segments under Software and Services, the largest revenue contributor is the LMR Communications.

LMR Communications (MCN from Q2 2025 onwards): This part of Software and Services makes up 60% of the sales from this segment. As MSI products have multi-year and even multi-decade lifespan, the longevity affords MSI plentiful opportunities and demand for upgrades, maintenance, repairs and replacement. The services provided for the LMR Communications also include technical support, preventive maintenance, cyber security, system updates.

The next two sub-segment under Software and Services each brought in 20% of the sales from this segment.

Where Do MSI Sell To?

MSI sells its products and services mainly in the United States, but a significant amount (28% to 31%) comes from international markets.

The US market is not just the larger piece of the revenue for MSI, it is also the faster growing segment. According to the 2024 10K (emphasis added):

Net sales (in FY 2024) increased by $839 million, or 8%, compared to 2023. The 10% increase in net sales within the Products and Systems Integration segment was driven by a 13% increase in the North America region and a 3% increase in the International region. The 5% increase in the Software and Services segment was driven by a 12% increase in the North America region partially offset by a 8% decline within the International region.

More about the "8% decline within the International region" in the Inversion Test segment later.

Can this business survive in an A.I. age?

Deploying artificial intelligence meaningfully in its products and services is key to MSI's competitive edge, and the company seems to be doing that.

At the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference, CFO Jason Winkler described the benefits of AI integration in APX NEXT, the many different ways Ai Assistance ViQi help users to keep their eyes up and stay focus on their tasks, assimilate different data sources to assist in report writing, provide information upon just voice commands (watch the videos of ViQi in action). This AI assist feature - and more - is not in some promised future; it is here and is available.

CEO Gregory Brown expressed confidence at the Q3 earnings call that,

We've made significant investments to integrate these new technologies in AI into our solutions and I anticipate this being a growth driver for the company for years to come.

Take, for instance, MSI's APX NEXT. CEO Brown expects 200,000 users to be subscribed to APX NEXT applications by the end of this year, and is projecting that number to reach 300,000 by the end of 2026.

Bearing in mind MSI an installed base of about 2 million first responders in the U.S., the growth opportunities afforded by just from APX NEXT is significant.

In short, the company will survive in an A.I. age; it continually invests in R&D and acquire companies that can value-add its eco-system.

Is The Business Doing Well?

Although MSI is a 97-year-old company, the MSI we know today has evolved. Referring to the 2010 10K, MSI was still selling handphones and set-top boxes for digital video, among many other things.

Gregory Brown was promoted to the CEO position in 2008 while the business was losing money. Under his leadership, in he split the company into Motorola Mobility (the cellphone side of the company) and Motorola Solutions, which he continue to lead as CEO till today, laser focused on serving governments (public safety and first responders) and enterprise customers (hospitality, retail, etc.).

Since the split, MSI has been growing revenue steadily, from $8.8 billion in FY 2012 to $10.8 billion by FY 2024. Honestly, that kind of growth is not impressive but that is a sign of a mature business that is still able to grow market share. It also signals persistent market demands for MSI's products and services, in spite of competition from other companies. Regardless of economic cycles, governments will still spend on public safety.

What is impressive is MSI net income growth, almost doubling from $0.878 billion to $1.577 billion in the same time period.

The main reason for this is the company's healthy and expanding margins. From 2012 to 2024, operating margins increased from 14.84% to 24.91%, while net margins expanded from 10.09% to 14.58%.

Another reason for the expanding margins would be the deliberate emphasis to generate more revenue from Software and Services. In 2018, the Software and Services segment was responsible for $2.2 billion, or 31%, of the company's total sales. By 2024, that has increased to 36% of total sales. While Products and Systems Integration segment grew by 35% from FY 2018 to FY 2024, the Software and Services segment grew by a massive 77%.

On a Q-on-Q basis (see Q3 2025 slides), MSI did well on most fronts as well, posting revenue growth of 6% in North America and 13% internationally, and revenue growth of 6% in Products and Systems Integration and 11% in Software and Services.

The -1.18% decline in Software & Services in FY 2024 is concerning, but as of Q3 2025 has rebounded to +11% growth, suggesting it may have been temporary (declining revenue from Ukraine, exiting business from UK, FX headwinds). The strong, +12.65%, 2024 YoY growth in Products and Systems Integration will help to drive more MCN Software and Services moving forward.

Concluding Level 1 Analysis

Motorola Solutions is a high-quality, mission-critical, margin-expanding business with demonstratively strong demand. MSI has a working AI integration strategy that is winning customers. It is not a fast grower, but a reliable compounder.

As MSI passed my Level 1 Analysis, I shall proceed to Level 2 Analysis. For that, I shall be using the P.R.I.M.E. Protocol, developed after my reflective piece.

Prevent: PASSED

At this stage, I am looking for events that can result in catastrophic losses for the company. Preventing myself from investing in companies facing high probability threats is a key part of risk management.

Inversion Test

This inversion filter draws from the wisdom of Charlie Munger. To pass this, a company must not face any existential crisis stemming from a single point of failure.

Remember the "8% decline within the International region" that was mentioned earlier? In 2022, UK’s Competition and Markets Authority (CMA) imposed a price cap on its Airwave network for emergency services to ensure a fairer deal for UK emergency services, intending to reduce costs by nearly $248 million per year.

In spite of this price cap, MSI continue to grow revenue through 2023 and 2024. MSI is not immune to government interventions but they have yet to break it.

MSI passes the Inversion Test.

Diversification Risk

MSI's revenue is spread across more than 100,000 public safety and enterprise customers in over 100 countries.

Yes, most of the revenue is concentrated in North America. Its customers ranged from the U.S. Navy to state level governments, signing both annual and multi-year contracts, giving MSI quantifiable order backlogs and revenue visibility. At the Q3 2025 conference call, CEO Brown described the nature of the contracts in this way,

In addition, we received 3 large orders during the quarter for P25 system upgrades to our new D-Series infrastructure, a $110 million order from the State of Colorado, an $84 million order from the Tennessee Department of Safety and an $82 million order from a U.S. state and local customer. These large multiyear orders are further testament to our customers' commitment to investing in our next-generation LMR infrastructure, and we have a large funnel of opportunities over the next several years.

Public safety is a necessity that cannot be simply terminated due to budget constraints.

Other than governments, MSI serves enterprise customers from multiple sectors, so there is no industry concentration risk.

MSI passes the Diversification Risk Test.

Regulatory Risk

MSI has not faced regulatory issues with the U.S. government, its largest customers. Plus, I hate to repeat myself, but the mission critical nature of MSI products and services mean its clients cannot simply terminate their contracts with MSI on a whim.

MSI passes the Regulatory Risk Test.

Competitive Moat Erosion and Technological Obsolescence Test:

Once MSI gets its sticky products into organizations, like its Command Centre solutions and software applications, switching cost is high.

Once investors understand the nature of many of the contracts, the moat is apparent. Through the way CFO Winkler described revenue recognition in the Q1 2023 conference call, it is clear that once a client is won over, it is difficult for competitors to wrestle that customer away. When a client is inside MSI's ecosystem, opportunities to upsell additional services and products continue to widen the moat,

... a $340 million federal IDIQ award to combine existing services and provide new LMR services to the U.S. Air Force, which has long been an important customer of ours.

Given the nature of this 10-year agreement, we recorded only $11 million of backlog in the first quarter and expect backlog to be recorded ratably over time for the remainder of the contract. Additionally, we expect new services included it to contribute an incremental $60 million of revenue over the 10-year period when compared to the previous service agreements.

MSI also built inflation escalators into its contracts. Q1 2024 earnings call CEO Brown explained how MSI improved gross margins,

... we have contracts that have appropriate cost of living mechanisms built into multiyear services contracts. And as they come due we will be disciplined in making sure we're recovering appropriate cost increases that come and hit the firm.

AI is a gain for MSI, and not just a productivity gain. The company has been spending aggressively on R&D, increasing its expenditure by more than 33% from FY 2020 to FY 2024, which increased at a higher rate compared to the 24% in the earlier 5-year period from 2016 to 2020. Product enhancements like AI Assist are gaining momentum.

MSI passes the Competitive Moat Erosion and Technological Obsolescence Test.

Resilience: PASSED

This is the financial fortress and margin stability test.

Although MSI has a significant amount of debt - and is not the negative net debt type company that gives me immediate peace of mind from solvency risk - its robust free cash flow that exceeds annual capital expenditure needs by 8.3 times. In a stress test scenario where operating cash flow declines drastically, MSI still has sufficient cash on hand to sustain another 3.5 years of CAPEX. Finally, with almost a billion dollars in cash, plus another $2.25 billion in its untapped revolving facility, MSI's ability to do bolt-on acquisitions is not constrained by liquidity, but more of management's consideration of its leverage ratio, and maintaining its investment grade credit rating.

For a company with strong free cash flow that amply covers its CAPEX, and a liquidity buffer of $894 million in cash and $2.25 billion in untapped revolving credit, I am not concerned, though I will keep an eye on the trend if current ratio edges towards 1.

As mentioned in an earlier segment, MSI is expanding margins meaningfully, evidence of a business that has done many things right - improving product mix in the shift towards higher margin Software and Services, pricing power, price escalators built into the contracts, and better monetization of its 2 million first responder installed base. Case in point will be the customers that purchased APX NEXT. Many add on a $300 annual subscription per device to get a number of mission-critical features, essentially converting an once-off hardware sale into a sticky, recurring, high-margin revenue stream.

This further reinforces MSI's moat characteristic.

CFO Winkler stated at the Q4 2024 conference call that MSI had paid 14 years of consecutive, double-digit increase. This is great news for dividend growth investors provided such increases are sustainable. MSI's conservative 30.6% FCF payout ratio, supported by a FCF growth rate that exceeds dividend growth rate, further signals to me that management is exercising a conservative capital allocation policy while decisively rewarding shareholders.

What I will keep in mind would be the declining dividend growth rate compared to its historical dividend growth rate. This is not a concern in terms of dividend sustainability; in fact it is a good thing as no one should expect that double-digit dividend growth rate to continue in perpetuity, and it shows that management is allocating capital thoughtfully.

MSI passes the Resilience Test.

As there is nothing that I could see that would Prevent me from investing in a financially Resilient company, MSI passes my P+R test meaning it is worth my while to find out its Intrinsic Value.

Intrinsic Value: FAILED

At the Q3 2025 earnings call, management maintained its FY 2025 guidance of 7.7% revenue growth and upped non-GAAP EPS guidance both at the top and bottom end, from the prior guidance of $14.88 to $14.98 to between $15.09 and $15.15 per share.

Based on my assumptions, and using analysts' estimates, MSI's historical probabilities of beating/meeting/failing forecasts, MSI's historical normal multiple ranges, I came to a probabilistic intrinsic value of $334.98. Based on my rules, I require a 10% discount to intrinsic value to consider buying and since MSI trades at a 11% premium, it failed this gate.

If I wanted an even wider margin of safety than 10%, or if I want to know when would MSI become a proverbial kitchen sink buy, it would be under $251 when MSI trades at a 25% discount to its probabilistic intrinsic value.

In other words, MSI is definitely not cheap and it is not a value-buy. What the 23% decline in price from its high of $500 in November 2024 to $378 as of 24 December 2025 does is to offer an entry into a high quality, mature compounder, at a more palatable buy.

MSI fails to have a margin of safety based on its Intrinsic Value.

Momentum: PASSED

MSI is currently trading above its 50 MA and has stopped making lower lows. Note that "passing" this does not guarantee that the price will not fall further.

MSI passes the Momentum Test; lower chances of this being a falling knife.

Management: NEUTRAL, LEAN Positive

Insiders hold 0.34% of total shares outstanding. No insiders has made significant buys since 2023; instead there has been steady selling of shares.

However, under the leadership of CEO Gregory Brown, MSI has been hailed as one of the greatest turnaround in corporate history, transforming a money-losing business into a mature growth company.

MSI scores neutral on Management Test.

Moat: PASSED

As discussed under Level 1 Analysis, MSI displays moat-like characteristics and scores well from this perspective.

I left the discussion of MSI's network effects to this segment as it is not obvious like Meta's direct to consumer social network. MSI exhibits data + physical + multi-platform networking effects.

A simple example would be a partnership between Motorola and Google (GOOGL) to allow Android users to share live video with 911 call handlers within seconds of connecting the call. This connects two platforms - MSI's 911 Command Center and Google’s Android Emergency Live Video. Command Center makes up a massive install base of around 60% of North America’s public safety answering points (PSAPs), which attracted a partner like GOOGL whose Android OS powers around 37% of mobile device users in North America. A fragmented market would not benefit from such an integration.

The more organizations - governments or enterprises - use MSI Command Center, the more people use Android devices, the greater the network effect.

Another example would be the Rave Mobile Safety. This is a panic button system that will notify staff and 9-1-1 with a single touch of a button, brings first responders on site faster and more informed, and provide protection all with the click of a button. Over 12,000 K12 schools in all fifty states have Rave installed. Customers are not restricted to North America. There are over 20,000 customers world wide.

As more organizations adopt Rave, and as more data is fed into MSI, this increases the value of MSI's solutions, and increases the switching costs for agencies that have already invested in MSI's systems.

These two examples demonstrate: Visual, audio and text data + physical devices + multi-platform networking effect in action.

MSI passes the Moat Test.

Execution: WEAK PASSED

The disconnect is valuation, not quality.

I consider MSI's business in the Super-SWAN category. This is a business that allows me to sleep well at night regardless of macro-economic issues. I would be happy to own more MSI and allocate it a 5%-8% position in my portfolio.

However, based on its current valuation, MSI would be at best a Weak Buy.

Readers who followed me since my piece on IDEXX Laboratories (IDXX) might have felt my anguish channeled through your laptop screen at my decision not to invest in that wonderful company because I wanted to wait for my margin of safety. Result: I missed a +76% gain over a mere 8 months.

This is one of the lessons I learned from my reflective piece, which is to pay fair value, or pay up a little, for a high quality business, and dollar-cost-average slowly. I was not willing to pay up that much when MSI was trading at $500 in November 2024 but an entry now is more acceptable to me, which was why I initiated a 1% position in MSI at $372 last week. As I am willing to own more of it at lower prices, my initial allocation for this holding left plenty of room to add if/when its price falls to support levels.

Risks

It will be remiss of me not to state risks that can befall any company, include one as good as MSI.

Competitors can adopt AI as well. It is highly probable that industry-wide AI integration will end up commoditizing its utility, so MSI's moat is never about its ability to utilize AI well, but lies in the integration of its various hardware and software across its products and services, and across platforms (like the partnership with GOOGL) - enhanced by AI - to create an attractive ecosystem where users find it difficult to extricate themselves from.

In the Moat discussion, MSI's Command Center market penetration of around 60% was highlighted as a good thing - and it is. The challenge with a large size, having the dominant market share, is sustaining a high growth rate that more normal for a smaller company. Having the dominant market share also implies a greater need, and more resources to be allocated to defend it. More on this below.

Earlier, I highlighted the 8% decline partially caused by a price cap in UK. The matter has come to a conclusion. However, with this as a precedent, other countries may follow suit in their efforts to control their national budget. No government can slash public funds set aside for mission critical services like safety but they can put a cap on what they are willing to pay. This may put a damper on growth expectations.

Investors have repriced MSI down from its high of $500 from just about 13 months ago to its current price of $378 as of close on 24 December 2025. Part of the reason could be their concerns about this once double-digit growth machine showing signs of slowing down. According to data from FactSet, the 10 analysts covering it are at best expecting high single digit growth rates.

Investors expecting double-digit revenue growth rates seen in 2015, 2016, 2017, 2021 and 2022 have to moderate their expectations.

Likewise, investors enamored with MSI multiple years of double-digit adjusted operated income growth rate (29% in 2015, 48% in 2016, 11% in 2017, 31% in 2018, 11% in 2019, 19% in 2021, 13% in 2022, 15% in 2023, and 16% in 2024) may find its expected future growth rate hard to accept for the price multiples they are paying, thereby putting the onus on sustaining the current stock valuation squarely on price multiple expansion. The alert investor would also need to take the EPS growth with some perspective as it is partly attributed to the declining share count. MSI has been buying back shares at a good pace. Since 2012, total shares outstanding has fallen by about 100 million, from 266 million in FY 2012 to 167 million as of Q3 2025.

When modeling MSI's intrinsic value, I have built in margin of safety in performing the probabilistic intrinsic value sensitivity check by reducing P/E multiples assigned to bull (23), base (18), bear (13) scenarios versus past 7-years normal P/E of 23. Investors who want to own MSI at the current price need to be prepared to own more of it at lower prices.

Final Thoughts Before Concluding Level 2 Analysis: Silvus

If MSI had continue to operate in the same industries, in the same way, it is likely that the company will plod along as a mature compounder.

That is, if not for its Silvus acquisition. That is a growth catalyst that could boost the company's growth into the double-digits zone again. I believe that the potential of the Silvus acquisition is something analysts have not fully come to appreciate. For instance, in an October 2025 note, Morningstar analyst Eric Compton have this to say about the Silvus acquisition,

Silvus provides mobile ad-hoc networking technologies, essentially software-defined mobile networks with lower infrastructure needs and the ability to operate in contested areas. This opens up new markets and capabilities in the broader MCN unit...

We view the $5 billion [correcting Eric: it should be $4.4 billion] Silvus acquisition as another example of Motorola executing well. The acquisition is set to check all the boxes, including TAM expansion and margin accretion, all with strategic overlap with Motorola's core networking and safety platform focus.

That is all true. And underwhelming. Let's unpack that.

MSI already caters to military needs in terms of voice data. MSI is the leader in LMR/P25. However, that technology is unable to handle the large amount of high bandwidth video data coming from swarms of drones or cameras mounted on dozens of assets. CEO Brown explained it as Silvus filling this gap as the leader in mission-critical-data.

We're [MSI] the leader in mission-critical voice through TETRA and P25. Now we're the leader in mission-critical data as defined by high-speed, low latency, mobile ad hoc networking. That's a great complement. As we envision -- as these new markets we're going after because Silvus gives us new market, new market in defense, new market in autonomous, new market in drone infrastructure, new market in manned. And they're the market leader,

Silvus has deep knowledge of the communication challenges and needs of the military, having done research work with DARPA, Office of Naval Research, U.S. Army, NSF, Navy and Air Force for more than a decade to solve problems. CEO Brown also shared that Silvus' technology is already installed in some defense and military drone platforms, including Anderol and AeroVironment, and it's certified with over 100 leading manufacturers.

In other words, Silvus technology have been field tested, has widespread adoption, has won customers, and has passed regulatory approvals.

With Silvus's technology, high-bandwidth video and sensor data could be sent back to the MSI's command center, to integrate with MSI's software to provide a single operational view.

On the financial side, CEO Brown estimates that the $3 billion TAM that Silvus operates in could double in the next 4 years; this could be the typical optimistic CEO-speak. What he was really confident about was that Silvus would contribute $500 million in revenue in FY 2025, with a growth target of 20% to $600 million in FY 2026, contributing $0.30 to $0.40 in EPS. $500 million of revenue within a $3 billion TAM - assuming that TAM is accurate - gives Silvus a 16%-17% market share, translating to plenty of room to grow.

Silvus' revenue contributions are not just from North America. Internationally, a NATO country has placed a $10 million order with MSI thanks to Silvus.

I am not doing an advertisement for Silvus, but it is really useful to get a deeper understanding of how its MN-MiMO technology, supported by Spectrum Dominance 2.0's suite of LPI LPD anti-jamming and advance threat protection capabilities, works together with its StreamCaster MANET radios to achieve more reliable connectivity, reduced power consumption, increased range, and lower probability of detection in any operational environment. The more StreamCaster MANET radios are added, the more nodes there are, the stronger and more resilient the mesh network becomes. This not only drives further adoption of StreamCaster MANET radios, it also prevents the entry of competitors' communication devices.

It is evident that the potential for the network effect to increase significantly if the different branches of the military adopt Silvus technology in order to have different assets communicate, to feed data back to a unified Command Centre for better decision making.

All the above points to Silvus strengthening MSI's moat.

In short, I recognize the role as a growth catalyst that Silvus would play, and it may just be the silver bullet that would jolt this mature compounder into a low-to-mid double-digit revenue compounder again. However, I do not intend to model in this addition growth as I do not wish to force my speculations - however well reasoned - into my valuation just to turn a Weak Buy into a Strong Buy.

Conclusion

Motorola Solutions (MSI) is a high-quality, margin-expanding, cash-generative business with sustainable, long-term advantages. The PRIME Protocol confirms MSI passed almost all the qualitative gates: no existential risks (Prevent), financial fortress (Resilience), not a falling knife (Momentum), strong moat (Moat), and proven management (Management).

However, MSI Intrinsic Value is unattractive now. At $378.38, it trades at a 11% premium. The market has spoken, believing that MSI is a solid mature growth growth company with expanding margins, durable business but it has lost the luster of the double-digit growth engine of prior years. Until the growth from Silvus becomes more prominent, the market may continue to view MSI as nothing more than that.

It has seen multiple compression over the past 13 months, and there is still be room for further price decline. Investors who cannot stomach another 20% drawdown should avoid MSI.

MSI is a business that I like for its high quality, SWAN-like characteristics. It is a suitable part of any long-term investor who is fine with short-term price fluctuations. Dividend growth investors with a long runway would also love MSI for its record of growing dividends at a double-digit rate for the past 14 years.

Strictly speaking, MSI is still trading at a premium and investors need to decide if MSI is a business worth paying up for. Remember, MSI is not a high growth story backed by AI hype. It is not a well-known company; only 22 thousand SA subscribers follow it; for comparison, 1.33 million SA subscribers follow Tesla (TSLA) and 1.32 million follow Microsoft (MSFT). This lack of attention (cough... popularity) to this steady compounder could just present an opportune entry point for someone willing to pay up for this quality company. For now, MSI would be a Weak Buy for me.

data.title: Endeavour Silver: Minimal Margin Of Safety At Current Levels
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold or silver). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. SEOs = silver-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day. UG = Underground. MTPA = million tonnes per annum. FS/DFS = Definitive Feasibility Study.

Introduction

Endeavour Silver (EXK) is a multi-asset producer with the bulk of its revenue coming from silver and four mines (one pending sale) in its portfolio. The bulk of the company's revenue comes from Mexico from its Guanacevi and Terronera Mines, with Endeavour Silver diversifying itself a little more this year, adding a polymetallic mine in Peru, Kolpa. The result is that Endeavour Silver should see production vault closer to the 10 million ounce mark for silver alone in 2026, trouncing its more recent high of ~5.96 million ounces in 2022.

Unfortunately, while the company has added a fourth mine, Terronera, the cost to build massively exceeded the initial budget, coming in at ~$339 million vs. initial projections of $230 million (+47% vs. budget) and an upward revised budget of $271 million. This was impacted by a stronger Mexican Peso than hoped, labor and commodity/equipment inflation, but also lengthy delays relative to guidance.

And while some investors might shrug this off and be glad that Terronera has finally reached commercial production with delays and overruns in the rear-view mirror, short-term and permanent damage has been done to the investment thesis. This includes a painful blow to its per share metrics on the back of ~72% share dilution on a fully diluted basis (~174 million shares to ~299 million shares) since its initial Terronera Feasibility Study was released in Q3'21, in addition to brutal hedges that have capped the price at which it's selling a large chunk of gold and some of its silver.

In fact, aside from gold forward sales that will affect ~55,000 ounces of future gold production over the next 22 months, with sales capped at $2,325/oz gold (~50% below spot), Endeavour also has zero cost collars for upwards of 900,000 ounces over the next year, capped its effective silver price at $42.00/oz on just shy of 10% of its silver production profile.

Hence, Endeavour Silver is not nearly as exposed to the recent surge in gold/silver prices as its peers and was one of the few companies of its scale still posting negative free cash flow in Q3'25. In this update we'll dig into the Q3'25 results, recent developments, and why Endeavour Silver's reward-risk setup is no longer favorable.

Q3 Production & Sales

Endeavour Silver released its Q3'25 results last month, reporting quarterly production of ~1.77 million ounces of silver and ~7,300 ounces of gold. This translated to a 102% increase in silver production year-over-year, partially offset by a 22% dip in gold production. Higher silver production benefited from the acquisition of the Kolpa Mine earlier this year in Peru, with ~598,700 ounces of silver produced in its first full quarter in Q3'25.

However, the material increase in silver output was largely related to lapping easy year-over-year comps because of the trunnion failure at Guanacevi in Q3'24 that affected its Q3'24 results, with just ~768,900 ounces produced in the year-ago period. As for gold production, Bolanitos has significantly underperformed expectations this year, producing just ~12,600 ounces through the first nine months vs. the company's annual guidance midpoint of 20,000 ounces. The lower grades year-over-year (1.36 G/T of gold vs. 2.0 G/T of gold) combined with lower gold grades at Guanacevi (1.07 vs. 1.46 G/T of gold) resulted in significantly lower gold output.

Guanacevi

Starting with its Guanacevi Mine, production came in at ~1.02 million ounces of silver and ~3,200 ounces of gold, up 33% and 13% from the year-ago period. However, the increase in production was entirely throughput-driven, benefiting from a return to more normal operating rates above 1,000 TPD while silver and gold grades slid 15% and 27%, respectively. Meanwhile, costs at the asset spiked further to $31.09/oz despite the increased denominator and despite lower sustaining capital spend in the period.

Digging into cost performance, the company noted that higher purchases of third-party material (requirement that at least 10% of throughput go through local miners from third-party purchases), with ~19,100 tonnes of purchased material vs. ~10,200 tonnes in Q3'24. Meanwhile, royalties also surged, with an effective ~14.6% royalty that makes some West African royalty rates look not so bad, with this related to the 16% royalty on sales when silver is above $25.00/oz on the El Porvenir/El Curso concessions, besides the $12/tonne fixed production payment.

On a positive note, silver prices made up for the higher unit costs, and we should see materially improved mine operating earnings at this primary silver mine in Q4'25 given the spike in silver prices. Still, this is an asset whose future remains less certain given the relatively small reserve base, with just ~460,000 tonnes of reserves at year-end 2024, translating to barely one year of mine life vs. its annual throughput rate of ~400,000 tonnes at an 1,100 TPD rate.

Kolpa

As for the company's Kolpa Mine, production came in at ~598,700 ounces of silver, ~5,700 tonnes of lead and ~3,700 tonnes of zinc, generating ~$39.0 million in revenue in the period, but a paltry ~$3.9 million in mine operating earnings. This was based on ~195,800 tonnes processed at an average grade of 105 G/T of silver, and 5.3% lead/zinc, slightly below M&I grades at the time of the acquisition at ~107 G/T of silver and ~6.1% lead/zinc.

As for cost performance, AISC came in at $30.31/oz, and should have landed significantly higher if the company were on track for its planned sustaining spend of $18.0 million this year, of which it's spent a mere ~$5.9 million. Even assuming just $7.0 million spending in Q4'25 to get closer to the full-year spend, I'd expect another high-cost quarter here, with this asset actually pushing Endeavour Silver's cost profile higher and diluting the benefit of what's expected to be lower cost ounces from Terronera.

Terronera

Moving to Endeavour's new Terronera Mine in Mexico, commercial production was declared in October, several months behind initial plans. As for September production, the first three weeks went well with ~39,200 tonnes processed before a circuit electrical problem in the SAG mill related to high humidity and condensation derailed processing for six days, with damage to pre-charge resistors. During this period, operations met the requirements to declare commercial production with the plant exceeding 90% of nameplate capacity (excluding downtime), with an average throughput of 1,866 TPD of a planned 2,000 TPD.

During the quarter, Terronera generated ~$31.5 million in pre-production revenue from the sale of ~212,700 ounces of silver and ~6,400 ounces of gold, and the company hopes to access higher-grade zones by mid-2026. However, those hoping for a transformative 2026 from a growth standpoint will be disappointed.

This is because the late start to commercial production and recent soft guidance of ~360,000 tonnes over the next six months at average grades of 122 G/T of silver and 2.52 G/T of gold at ~74% for gold and ~85% for silver and ~97% payability implies production of barely 1.15 million ounces of silver and ~21,000 ounces of gold, well below grades previously expected in Year 1 (3.98 G/T of gold and 236 G/T of silver) as the company ensures things are running smoothly and it can work out any kinks.

To be clear, this period of sending lower grades through the plant is normal for any operation and not a huge deal for the big picture. However, if the ramp-up was even remotely on schedule, this would have been completed this year, setting up Terronera for a full year of planned grades in 2026 vs. what's expected to be a much softer H1'26 based on these parameters. To summarize, with much lower gold production than previously expected in 2026 and over 2,830 ounces of gold being sold at $2,325/oz because of hedges put in place as part of its Terronera financing, 2026 will be a much weaker year than initially anticipated, with the impact of these hedges continuing through the first half of 2027.

Financial Results

Finally, as for Endeavour's financial results, revenue came in at ~$111 million (+109% year-over-year), operating cash flow improved to ~$27 million (+219% year-over-year), but free cash flow was still negative, with Endeavour being one of the few producers of its scale still posting negative free cash flow. Fortunately, metal prices have moved materially higher since the Q3'25 results, and Terronera will see a higher contribution (assuming no more setbacks), with quarter-to-date gold and silver prices averaging upwards of $4,100/oz and $52.00/oz.

Still, investors need to remember that Endeavour's silver collars cap a portion of its silver production at $42.00/oz (meaning it doesn't benefit fully from this recent $30.00/oz rally above these levels) and over half of its gold production is being sold into forward sales at $2,325/oz, leaving it $2,000/oz short of spot levels. To summarize, Endeavour Silver is not nearly as leveraged as it may appear on the surface and will be much less of a beneficiary of higher prices in 2026 than its average non-hedged silver producer peer.

Recent Developments

Bolanitos Mine Sale

On a positive note, Endeavour has managed to sell its short-life and non-core Bolanitos Mine to the silver space's marginal asset collector, Guanacevi Silver (GSVRF), a company which has an uncanny knack for buying tired assets at too-high prices that struggle in most commodity price environments.

If closed, the sale will bring in upfront cash of $30 million and $10 million in Guanajuato Silver shares, as well up to $10 million in contingent payments ($5 million each x2), if the mine produces 2 million and four million silver-equivalent ounces. Overall, I think this deal makes sense for the company given that Bolanitos' short life and scale, with it no longer being core to the portfolio and significantly underperforming expectations this year (sitting at just 63% of annual guidance midpoint as of Q3'25).

Convertible Debt Issuance

Earlier this month, Endeavour Silver announced the closing of its offering of $350 million in unsecured convertible senior notes (maturing in 2031, 0.25% interest rate), with an initial conversion price of US$12.45. These notes would result in ~28 million additional shares being issued, if converted, contributing to nearly 10% share dilution on top of what's already been a busy year for share dilution with ATM usage (~2.64 million shares sold at US$5.81) and a share sale of ~11.3 million shares at US$3.88.

On a positive note, these notes come at a much lower interest rate than its senior secured debt facility with ING Capital LLC, with these notes able to fully repay this higher-interest debt facility and also be used towards Pitarilla development, assuming it's approved. Overall, the re-financing makes sense, it's just unfortunate it had to come with what's likely to be more dilution, piling more shares on top of what has been one of the most rapidly rising share counts in the producer space over the past five years.

Kolpa Mine

As for operations at the Kolpa Mine in Peru, Endeavour Silver noted that grades were slightly below expectations in Q3'25, offset by higher throughput, but which obviously impacted unit costs given increased tonnes processed required to produce the same number of ounces. Endeavour Silver shared that cash costs were above the historical trend on site and its expectations, coming in at $16.43/oz (AISC of $30.31/oz). To date, Endeavour Silver has spent just ~$5.9 million of a planned $18.0 million on sustaining capital this year (~33% of planned annual spend with one quarter to go), implying at least moderate catch-up and what will likely be higher AISC quarter-over-quarter.

As for work on the expansion to 2,500 TPD being considered, Endeavour Silver noted it continues to complete work to ensure there are sufficient economies of scale at the mine level, with it hoping to make a final decision on this by January. As it stands, the asset is operating at just over 2,000 TPD, with a ~20% expansion having the potential to push annual silver production closer to ~2.7 million ounces per year, assuming ~90% recoveries and an average grade of 100 G/T of silver.

Pitarilla

Finally, on future growth aspirations and Pitarilla, Endeavour Silver has noted that it hopes to produce 30 million silver-equivalent ounces by 2030, implying nearly 100% growth from current levels. This suggests an acquisition could be in the cards at some point given that Pitarilla alone may not get it to that level, and while it's nice to have significant growth aspirations, I much prefer companies that target per share growth vs. an arbitrary growth number, especially based on what we saw from Terronera which was limited per share benefit to building because of the avalanche of new paper dropped on shareholders.

As for Pitarilla's scale, the company confirmed that it is looking at a relatively large UG operation at 3,000 to 4,000 TPD with grades that could run at closer to 300 G/T silver-equivalent. Assuming the low end of this range (3,000 TPD), Pitarilla could contribute ~9 million payable silver-equivalent ounces, translating to upwards of 50% production growth. However, capex for a UG operation was ~$277 million in 2009, and I would be surprised to see actual costs come in below $500 million, making this nearly twice the planned upfront capex of Terronera.

So, while Pitarilla certainly provides growth, it's difficult to be optimistic about the per share benefit from Pitarilla if a smaller project nearly led to 80% share dilution during a bull market for metals when this is a larger project. In summary, while growth is exciting for most companies, it's difficult to be excited about this growth opportunity given the cost it has come at for Endeavour Silver shareholders in the past, nevermind the massive cost-overruns and delays for a smaller project in Terronera.

Valuation

Based on ~299 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$10.00, Endeavour Silver trades at a market cap of ~$2.99 billion and an enterprise value of ~$3.1 billion (as of end of Q3'25, plus $30 million assumed cash proceeds from Bolanitos sale). This leaves the stock trading at just over ~10x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates using more conservative metals price assumptions next year ($58.00/oz silver, $3,900/oz gold), a multiple that I would argue leaves Endeavour fully valued based on where a company with its lack of Tier-1 jurisdiction exposure, heavy concentration to Mexico and poor track record of per share growth.

So, what's a fair value for the stock?

Using base case metal price assumptions, which I prefer to use for conservatism ($3,900/oz gold price in 2026, $56.00/oz silver in 2026), and what I believe to be generous multiples of 11.0x FY2026 cash flow estimates and 1.4x P/NAV (8% discount rate) for a relatively small scale silver-gold producer, I see an updated fair value for EXK of US$9.65. This suggests that the stock is fully valued at current levels, and given that I generally prefer a minimum 35% discount to fair value to justify starting new positions or adding to positions, I don't see any margin of safety here for EXK, with its ideal buy zone coming in below US$6.50/share.

Obviously, there's no guarantee that EXK has to head to these levels to provide a low-risk swing-trading opportunity, and it could continue to levitate if silver prices remain strong. However, as the saying goes, one doesn't need to swing at every pitch, and I prefer to focus on higher-quality producers trading at deep discounts to fair value with a superior track record of delivery on guidance, and ideally per share growth as well.

Hence, if EXK were the only publicly traded precious metals stock, being this picky might be a conundrum. However, with nearly producers in the sector to choose from, and EXK having a bottom quartile track record, my preference is to pay the right price or pass entirely, and I don't see chasing above US$9.80 as a compelling trade idea by any means.

So, what about EXK's fair value at higher metals prices?

Using less conservative metals prices of $65.00/oz silver and $4,400/oz gold, Endeavour Silver trades at closer to ~8.4x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates, a multiple that leaves the stock more than reasonably valued with room for upside. However, betting on the stock with these assumptions means that one must take a view that gold/silver prices barely correct from current levels and put up another strong year in 2026. And while this is certainly possible, silver prices are now far more extended than they were in August 2020 (preceding a ~26% drawdown over the next four months) and with a similar level of over-extension to early April 2011 (preceding a ~31% drawdown in the next six months).

Obviously, this time could be different, but with sentiment for silver running the hottest it has in years (89% bulls as of Thursday's close, implying nearly 9 bullish market participants for every bearish market participant), I think it's best to model conservative assumptions and take upside in metals as a bonus vs. buy names that need silver prices to stay above $60.00/oz medium-term to be materially undervalued.

Besides, there continue to be producers trading at dirt-cheap multiples elsewhere in the market, such as Alkane Resources (ALKEF) operating out of purely Tier-1 ranked jurisdictions (Sweden, Australia), and trading at just ~6.1x calendar year 2026 EV/FCF multiples as a gold-antimony producer (EXK: ~10.3x FY2026 EV/FCF estimates at same assumptions), using lower metals price assumptions ($3,900/oz gold) next year.

Hence, for those that are resource-agnostic (willing to own gold producers vs. solely silver producers), and looking for relative value in the sector with a lower risk profile (superior jurisdictions), one of the best 10-year track records of beating guidance vs. its peer group and a much stronger balance sheet (~$120 million in net cash to exit year-end 2025), I see Alkane Resources as one of several more attractive options for putting new money to work in the sector on any weakness.

Summary

Endeavour Silver reported another mediocre quarter in Q3'25 with costs up materially year-over-year (AISC climbed to $30.53/oz), and lower production than planned at its legacy Guanacevi and Bolanitos mines. And while higher gold and silver prices plus contribution from Terronera will help its Q4'25 results, painful hedges that cap some of its exposure at $2,325/oz gold and $42.00/oz silver shave off some of the leverage here and will remain in place through 2026 and through the first half of 2027 on its gold hedges.

Further, the impact of these gold hedges is now far more significant with the sale of Bolanitos, a gold-dominant asset (FY'25 guidance: ~20,000 ounces) that was at least helping to increase its weighted average realized gold price with the gold forward sales divided over a larger denominator. However, perhaps most importantly from an investment standpoint, the transformational growth expected from Terronera may have panned out on a production standpoint, little benefit will be seen from a per share standpoint.

This is because the ~90% growth in silver-equivalent production (relative to 2025 guidance) has largely been offset by over 70% share dilution the past four years, with more shares sold under the ATM in Q3'25 (~2.64 million shares at US$5.81/share), and the potential for closer to 90% share dilution when including the recent issuance of convertible debt (up to an additional 28 million shares of dilution).

And while investors moving money out of dollars getting de-based might think they're brilliant for parking money in precious metals, that thesis only works if one holds the metal itself (can't be printed) or holds miners that are holding the line or growing per share metrics, not seeing them decline. In fact, holding a precious metals producer that has seen over 190% share dilution in a bull market (year-end 2015 to year-end 2025) isn't much better than holding dollars that are also losing purchasing power, especially if that dilution hasn't resulted in production per share growth.

Unfortunately, in Endeavour Silver's case, its per share metrics are among the worst sector-wide, and this is exactly what they own. Further, while the carrot on the stick, Pitarilla, and the 30 million SEOs by 2030 goal point to a significantly higher market cap, it's difficult to be confident in the flow through on an actual per share and share price basis when a small project like Terronera blew out the share count to this degree during a bull market.

To summarize, Endeavour Silver offers minimal margin of safety at current levels using more conservative pricing, and while bringing Terronera online is a game-changer from a production standpoint, little benefit has been seen from a per share standpoint. Additionally, given the destruction of per share metrics in four short years during a bull market building a small project, I think it's difficult to be optimistic about the company's ability to grow per share metrics as it gears up to consider approving a larger project, Pitarilla.

Hence, with industry-lagging per share metrics and EXK now being back on a short-term technical sell signal above US$10.00 with much better relative value bets elsewhere, I don't see any way to justify chasing the stock here above US$10.00.

data.title: EFAA: International Diversification For Options Income Investors
data.description:
EFAA strategy

Invesco MSCI EAFE Income Advantage ETF (EFAA) is an actively managed derivative income fund launched on 07/17/2024. EFAA has a 12-month trailing yield of 7.93%, a 30-day SEC yield of 6.24% and an expense ratio of 0.39%. Distributions are paid monthly. As described in the prospectus by Invesco, the fund has an objective of total return through current income and long-term capital growth, with:

  • an equity portfolio tracking the MSCI EAFE Index;
  • equity-linked notes (ELNs) embedding options income strategies;
  • a buffer of cash or cash equivalents.

The underlying equity index is a developed markets index excluding the U.S. and Canada. The ELNs held by the fund are hybrid derivatives combining the characteristics of investing in stocks and options. They are senior, unsecured debt issued by financial institutions and typically embed covered call and/or cash secured put strategies. The embedded options strategies generate income and may partly hedge against a decline in the underlying assets. On the downside, they may reduce capital gains, depending on market conditions. Outsourcing options trading keeps the expense ratio low, but costs are passed through ELNs. I will use as a benchmark the MSCI EAFE Index, represented by iShares MSCI EAFE ETF (EFA).

Portfolio

EFAA holds the equity securities of the MSCI EAFE Index at approximately the same relative weights as in EFA, with an exposure of 77.3%. Additionally, it has 19.8% of assets in an Invesco money market fund, and 2.7% in 20 ELNs issued by nine banks. Diversification of ELN issuers dilutes counterparty risk and reduces dependency.

The MSCI EAFE Index has a focus on Europe (about 64% of asset value), but the heaviest country is Japan (22.1%), followed by the U.K. (14.8%).

The index has significant exposure in financials (25.2%) and industrials (19.1%).

EFAA is very diversified, with low company risk. The top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent only 10.1% of asset value, and the largest position weighs 1.55%.
